title: PATTERNS OF SEXUAL VULNERABILITY AMONG ADOLESCENTS AND YOUTHS IN SRI LANKA
abstract: To elucidate the level of sexual awareness, behaviour and vulnerability experienced by adolescents and youths in Sri Lanka, data were collected from an island-wide survey of 4004 unmarried and married persons aged 10–24. Most 10–14-year-olds do not possess sufficient knowledge on sexual behaviour, or hesitate to acknowledge it due to prevailing cultural barriers. Females show less knowledge on sexual behaviour, while those in the estate sector indicate the lowest awareness. Respondents aged 15–17 did not report a sound knowledge on sexuality. Of the unmarried urban males aged 18–24, nearly half believed that their peers had already experienced sexual intercourse, while the corresponding proportion of females was only 19 per cent. These estimates are significantly higher than the figures reported for the 15–17 age group of urban males (31 per cent) and females (12 per cent). Among those who had premarital sex, the majority had it with their lover; nevertheless, a substantial proportion of adolescents and youths may be vulnerable to contracting sexually transmitted infection (STI), including HIV/AIDS as they had potentially high-risk persons as sex partners. Comprehensive awareness programmes on sexual health are needed in order to lower their vulnerability.
authors_text: Indralal De Silva, W.; Karunathilake, K.; Perera, Roy
